user.name: Alma
user.bio: Privat investor & speculator | Mathematics | Self-made quant | Market researcher | Options and Futures trader

comment.body:
Alma - Fascinating and very informative info, as usual.  Firstly, thank you for doing this service.  It's freaking awesome.  

Your points about market liquidity, bets on vol, bets on upside vol, downside vol, etc. make sene to me with one exception, the market maker influence... for example, how charm will impact the market?   Doesn't the options bets inherently influence the amount of liquidity due to required MM hedging (e.g. charm impact, vanna impact, etc), and more over, the reflexivity of people reacting to the perceived charm impact?  That's not to say that this is the leading driver for the liquidity in the first place as actual sentiment (relative to fair value, etc.) could and does override these MM forces... but I have always thought that MM behavior + the related human/trading reflexivity can (and sometimes does) drive the mechanical moves which drives spot?  

Like an analogy could be... the options market bets end up creating a metaphorical unique in the moment/daily/weekly/monthly "maze" for liquidity to flow through or bump into dead ends at times, but these option bets are not the object traveling through the maze -- that's the liquidity finding its path to fair value.  Market makers behavior is a moving floor which changes direction depending on the part of the maze, and sometimes is even downhill or uphill that causes the maze to be easier/more difficult to get through.  The trick for quants is to try and visualize "the expected maze", the direction the floor will be moving at different times, the thickness of the walls of the maze, the speed of the floor changing etc.  Through that visualization of the perceived map, you can a theory of how the market may react... but the maze is not the market, the maze may just have very wide hallways with a fast floor at times, or vice versa.  Silly analogy, but that's how I think of it, but...

 I'm starting to wonder if I lean too much into the greek side based on your post...

comment.body: one cardinal thing that I have to correct instantly, is that do not think in "maze", think in a skewed Galton board, where the pegs aren't as well placed like they originally are, and there can be pegs that form a local "wall"... also their material is different, so the way the beads bounce from them have different speed

comment.body:
there are billions of people around the world, even more orders, strats, etc. with different weight/impact on the spot.
and when a new information appears (news, new price level, leakage, new local dynamics, etc.) everybody starts to react on it on its own way (mostly by assuming how the whole markets gonna react), but then slowly all these different behaviors start to converge, and create a new unit... and liquidity is born

comment.body:
it was pretty tough, we modelled different strats, momentum strats, mean reversion strats, etc.
but it was unnatural. then a prof told us to make it random. and when the order flow became random, the simulation looked way more market-like, and also it provided the patterns that quants and even technical traders observe
